# **Spot Or Not**

Status: *Approved*  Author: [Yi Chen](mailto:chen.y@salesforce.com)   Created: Feb 16, 2024  Last Updated: Feb 19, 2024

#

[Feb 15, 2024 \#escal-data](#feb-15,-2024-#escal-data)

[F.A.Q](#f.a.q)

[Why should we set spark.executor.cores to 4?](#why-should-we-set-spark.executor.cores-to-4?)

[What instance type do you recommend to replace the on-demand nodes?](#what-instance-type-do-you-recommend-to-replace-the-on-demand-nodes?)

[How many instances do we need for TASK nodes?](#how-many-instances-do-we-need-for-task-nodes?)

# 

# Goals {#goals}

**Purpose of this doc:** 

- Set a baseline for the cost benefit analysis of using spot instances in our EMR clusters.  
- Identify potential immediate and short-term solutions to bend the curve for our EMR expense.  
- Chart out future opportunities and directions to manage EMR and Spark costs.

# Summary {#summary}

- Spot instances do not have a cost advantage for EMR compared to alternatives.  
  - **Our EMR clusters are mostly only able to acquire the least desirable instance types that give us a low memory-to-cpu ratio of 1.9 ([prod-data-platform](#spot-instances-in-data-platform-emr6-clusters), [prod-dma-emr6](#prod-dma-emr6), and [backfill-dma-emr6](#backfill-dma-emr6)), which means an instant [waste of 50%-90%](#why-does-the-memory-to-cpu-ratio-matter-for-the-ec2-instance-types?)  because the minimum memory-to-cpu ratio for all our Spark jobs is 4\.**   
  - [**We are paying the most in dollar amount for the most undesirable instance types**](#what’s-the-breakdown-of-different-types-of-spot-instances-we-are-paying-now?) **with the most waste, so replacing them means large cost savings.**  
  - **In very rare situations our clusters may be able to acquire desirable instances with memory-to-cpu ratio of 7.9, [we are paying the full on-demand price without any spot discount](#prod-etc-emr6) for most of these instances. This also means an instant 50% waste if we can cover these instances under the Savings Plan.**  
- Besides no cost advantage of using spot instances, it also results in bad developer experience for losing debugging logs, [risks missing SLA, unnecessary slow-down of jobs,  and unnecessary EMR management cost](#what-are-other-issues-we-are-facing-by-using-spot-instances?). 

I recommend the following steps:

1. **Immediately** stop bidding any spot instances with less than a memory-to-cpu ratio of 4 (e.g.. c6gn family instances), this is because our Spark jobs in EMR have the **minimum** [ratio of 4](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/blob/master/emradm/data/emr/base.json#L134) and any instance types with less than this ratio means “instant waste.” **Immediately** work with AWS for ODCR so we can reserve enough instances with the desirable memory-to-cpu ratio because of the long lead time.   
2. **In the short-term**, consider [replacing the 2000 capacity units of spot instances of unpredictable type, with 200 on-demand r6g.16xlarge instances covered by Savings Plan and ODCR](#bookmark=kix.8mo2kanr412t). This allows us to shrink each of these cluster sizes by 80%. This change can double the cluster throughput (because the same amount of cores will actually be used). If this change is proven to be reliable, we can [further reduce the cluster size](#bookmark=id.yyatgjxdfmlb) with significant savings.  
3. **In the medium-term**, after shrinking the cluster size by 90%, we are left with three very small clusters (data-platform-emr6), each with only about 300 instances (assuming we will also replace the CORE nodes with 16xlarge instances instead of the current small instances).   
   1. We will consider merging them all together into one cluster, [enabling HA features by running 3 master nodes](https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2019/04/amazon-emr-announces-support-for-multiple-master-nodes-to-enable-high-availability-for-EMR-applications/), root-causing and fixing issues that are causing frequent cluster rotation. I estimate this can save another 30% cost (based on past anecdotal experience). This effort also involves improving the way we manage queues.  
   2. After merging the clusters into one HA-enabled data-platform-emr6, we will consider stopping auto-healers during business hours gradually so we can fix the root causes. Reducing the number of automatic cluster rotation can save another 10% cost.  
4. **In the long-term**, apply the lessons learned across all EMR clusters. Continue to improve Spark and EMR observability to enable data-driven decision making on tuning clusters, queues, and applications. We will further increase CPU utilization by oversubscribing CPU for Spark jobs, which can result in further estimated 20% savings.

# Context {#context}

In the past 3 months, Data Infrastructure costs have been increasing. Using January 2024 cost as an example, the EC2 related costs account for 76% of the total cost attributed to the Data Infrastructure team. Figuring out a way to save the EC2 cost will have a significant financial impact for Slack. In the short term, we should look for quick actions that can result in large returns (in savings) to bring the EMR cost back in control. In the long term, we need to invest in better metrics and profiling datasets to help us identify and prioritize cost efficiency projects.

![][image1]

# The Economy of Spot Instances {#the-economy-of-spot-instances}

## Spot Instances in data-platform-emr6 clusters {#spot-instances-in-data-platform-emr6-clusters}

In data-platform-emr6, more than 95% of task nodes are running c6gn.\* instance types. These instances have a memory-to-cpu ratio of 1.9. This is extremely low, because our default memory-to-cpu ratio is 4, and because our current hard-coded 1-core per container, when the users run into Spark application reliability issues, the only option is to tune the container memory. In the case studies, the ratio of the jobs that are impacted have a ratio of 20 and 16 respectively. With instances at 1.9 ratio,  it means that 93% of the CPUs on task nodes are doing nothing and wasted.. We are paying $1.169 hourly rate for c6gn.12xlarge. With the on-demand hourly rate of $2.0736, we are saving about 44%. Spot instances cannot be covered by Savings Plans, and Savings Plans in general can promise 50% savings against on-demand cost. We can reach the following conclusions:

- **The spot instance fleet does not save us money, compared to the option of paying the same instance type but covering them with a Savings Plan.** Not only that, with on-demand instances, we will solve [a bunch of reliability and SLA issues caused by spot fleet](#what-are-other-issues-we-are-facing-by-using-spot-instances?)..   
- **The above conclusion is assuming we are paying on-demand c6gn.\* instances, which still generate 90% of CPU waste for some critical workload. If we can pay on-demand instances with better memory-to-cpu ratio, and assuming we can reduce the CPU waste from 90% to a more reasonable level, we should obtain immediate cost savings despite not using spot instances.** 

The following screenshot shows the dominant instance types we are running for data-platform-emr6 clusters today (Feb 16, 2024).

![][image2]

The following is the “Used Vcores” for the data-platform-emr6 clusters in the past 24-hour window. Link [here](https://grafana.tinyspeck.com/d/BiIDMnAZz/emr?orgId=1&var-cluster=prod-data-platform-emr6&var-cluster=prod-data-platform2-emr6&var-cluster=prod-data-platform3-emr6&var-role=task&var-interval=1m&from=now-24h&to=now&viewPanel=112).

![][image3]

We can see each cluster’s task nodes maxed out with 12k used vcores, and the baseline bottom is at 3k vcores. Assuming we have 2000 nodes of c6gn.12xlarge running for all 3 clusters at peak, we should have a total of 48x2000= 96000 vcores. At peak usage of 12k vcores, **we are wasting 88% of all vcores for the task fleet**.

Another observation from this chart, is that over the 24-hour window, I do not see obvious peaks and valleys in the total task vcore usage. Especially the data-platform2-emr6: the usage of vcores across this period is stable in a smaller range. This means that it is a low risk to convert these spot instances for TASK nodes to appropriate on-demand instance types, and if we choose the instance type carefully, we expect to both save money and have better reliability.

## Spot Instances ROI in other clusters {#spot-instances-roi-in-other-clusters}

### *prod-dma-emr6* {#prod-dma-emr6}

This cluster is configured to have a max size of 1500\. We are running more than 1000 c7g.16xlarge instances, with 64 vCore, 122 GiB memory each, with a low memory-to-cpu ratio of 1.9. We are paying the spot price of $1.001/hr for these instances. The on-demand hourly rate of this instance type is $2.3123. This means that we are getting a 56% discount for using this instance. The following screenshot shows the spot instance price we are paying. A random sampling of the 10 current running applications in this cluster shows that 7 of them are using 4GB memory, 1 uses 12 GB (application\_1707950959448\_10107), 2 apps are using 20GB (application\_1707950959448\_10090 and application\_1707950959448\_10092). This matches the analysis above with the same conclusion: we are better off paying for on-demand instances that offer a memory-to-cpu ratio of at least 4\. 

![][image4]

### *Backfill-dma-emr6* {#backfill-dma-emr6}

There is no surprise here either. The backfill-dma-emr6 has acquired a lot of c6g.4xlarge instances, paying $0.250/hr spot prices. Compared to the full on-demand hourly rate of $0.544, we are getting a discount of 54%, which has no advantage of paying for on-demand instances with a Savings Plan. Similarly, these instances have 16 vCore and 30.5 GB memory, which offers a low memory-to-cpu ratio of 1.9. 

![][image5]

### *Prod-etc-emr6* {#prod-etc-emr6}

This cluster is able to acquire a mix of r6g, c6g, r7g family instances. The following table shows the spot price we are paying, on-demand price, our discount, and memory-to-cpu ratio. 

**Note that in this cluster we were able to get a large number of desirable instances from the r6g family, but we are paying full price with zero discount. We are however getting a small number of r7g instances with desirable instances with a desirable discount of 44% (which has no advantage compared to Savings Plans).** 

|  | Spot price we pay | on-demand price | discount | Memory-to-cpu ratio (the higher the better) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| r6g.2xlarge | $0.403 | $0.403 | 0% | 7.6 |
| c6g.16xlarge | $1.052 | $2.176 | 52% | 1.9 |
| r7g.2xlarge | $0.239 | $0.4284 | 44% | 7.6  |

# ![][image6]

# 

# Capacity Planning for Task Nodes {#capacity-planning-for-task-nodes}

The next question is if SPOT does not save money, what are our options.

## Data-platform-emr6 {#data-platform-emr6}

Using data-platform-emr6 clusters as an example. Ideally we should have historical metrics in the data warehouse that allow us to make data-driven decisions. In this section, we will use the task node charts over a 7-day period for the planning purpose. The following chart shows the 7-day spot instance allocated vcores across all data-platform-emr6 clusters  
![][image7]

From this chart, we can visually identify the medium watermark of 12k vcores per cluster (see the red line). That is, over the 7 days having 12k vcores per cluster (or 36k total) would satisfy the workload for 90% of the time. This translates to 187 instances of **r6g.16xlarge** type. In another word, **instead of 2000 spot instances of unpredictable types with no cost advantage, we should consider replacing them with 200 r6g.16xlarge on-demand instances covered by Savings Plans and ODCR.**

**Furthermore, we already know that these 12k vcores are at least 85% wasted because of the low memory-to-cpu ratio Spot instances, replacing by core count with higher ratio instances, means that these 200 r6g.16xlarge will offer at least double capacity for our workload. Put it in another word, we estimate that we only need to replace the 2000 spot instances with 100 r6g.16xlarge instances, which translates to large savings.**

## Backfill Clusters {#backfill-clusters}

Backfill workload is special because they are spiky, and we may want to scale down the cluster when there are no workload. Since the current spot instance configuration gives us instant waste of 50%, we can simply replace them with the more desirable on-demand instances (e.g. r6g, r7g). Even though they are twice as expensive, they are also twice less waste, so it should break even in cost, but we will get away without the headaches of spot instance issues. There is no point bidding for these desirable instance types, because (1) if we win the bid, we are most likely to pay the full price without discount and (2) why do we bid this instance type at the same time our other cluster wants this on-demand? It is possible that we will lose the spot instances to ourselves when we scale up another cluster with on-demand needs for the same instance (e.g. when we rotate the cluster)

# Case Study {#case-study}

## Feb 14, 2024 \#escal-data {#feb-14,-2024-#escal-data}

Context:  [https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1707955563809569](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1707955563809569)

User complains about cluster capacity for prod-data-platform3-emr6 cluster. The ResourceManager shows 93% of total resources used at 5pm. At this moment, the cluster capacity has maxed out with 200 unit CORE nodes, and 2002 units task nodes (spot). The core nodes are using r6g.2xlarge instance type, each with 8 vCore and 61 GiB memory. **The 2002 task nodes are mostly running c6gn.12xlarge instances, with 48 vCore, 91.5GiB memory. The memory-to-cpu ratio of task nodes is 1.9.**

For this impacted Spark application, it is configured to use 1 vCore and 16GB heap memory per Spark executor. This comes to a **memory-to-vcore ratio of 16**. The non-heap memory configuration is small so we ignore it here for simplicity.

Compare the Spark executor’s memory-to-cpu ratio, it means we will waste more than 50% of CPUs on the core nodes, and more than 85% of CPUs on the task nodes. Essentially, with a much higher memory-to-cpu ratio requirement of this application, it is struggling to find resources available from YARN’s perspective,  even though a large portion of the resources are wasted.

## Feb 15, 2024 \#escal-data {#feb-15,-2024-#escal-data}

Context: [https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1707955563809569](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1707955563809569)

YARN resource usage is at 93%-97% for all data-platform-emr6 clusters. Data ingestion job runs very slow. An hourly job took 7 hours to run. The [chart](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1707956995220339?thread_ts=1707955563.809569&cid=C01FB307Z7A) shows that the data-platform3-emr6 only uses 10% of memory and 7% of the CPU. This is because grafana is showing aggregated OS-level metrics, while YARN only has visibility to the resources at the container level. 

Due to the significant waste, and the limit of the cluster size (2002 task nodes and 200 core nodes all maxed out), the user’s application is not able to acquire resources fast enough. This application requires memory-to-cpu ratio of 20, so it requires a huge amount of available memory to be able to allocate containers, but the low ratio provided by the physical spot instances makes that a difficult task.  As a result, it took more than 10 minutes to launch the first 20 tasks (out of more than 5000 tasks).  

As a short-term solution, we recommend the user to change spark.executor.cores to increase the throughput of their application despite having no control on the cluster-level environment.

# 

# F.A.Q {#f.a.q}

## What are other issues we are facing by using spot instances? {#what-are-other-issues-we-are-facing-by-using-spot-instances?}

* **We suffer from delayed task launch speed, and risk missing SLA for critical jobs**. With Dynamic Allocation enabled, it takes time to allocate new Spot instances when we need to. For example, in the case study, the cluster is at max capacity (2002 task nodes), [it took 10 minutes to just launch the first 20 tasks of a 5000-task stage of an hourly Spark app](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1708024842153239?thread_ts=1707955563.809569&cid=C01FB307Z7A). It is difficult to know if this 10 minutes is caused by waiting for executors (which can be known after we improve our metrics), but it is possible that a large job will spend significantly time waiting for acquiring new spot capacity after the application has started.   
* **We lose important YARN and Spark logs from spot instances that were taken away**. This is a well-known user experience complaint among Spark users at Slack. Each spot instance hosts its own copy of the YARN logs. When the instance is gone, the logs are gone too. There are archived logs in S3, but those are not easily accessible and also not practical for Spark end users.   
* **Frequent node interruptions with large cluster size create cluster reliability problems.** The data-platform-emr6 cluster frequently runs at max capacity of 2000 task nodes, with only 1 master node. Frequent spot interruption creates unnecessary workload for the ResourceManager, which can contribute to many unnecessary automatic cluster rotation (through our auto-healer).   
* **We pay a per-node fee to AWS that’s called EMR Management Cost.** We pay more if we use a larger number of physical nodes. With current spot configuration, we are not limiting the instance size, so we will end up paying more EMR cost (although at a relatively lower portion compared to EC2 cost)

## 

## What’s the breakdown of different types of spot instances we are paying now? {#what’s-the-breakdown-of-different-types-of-spot-instances-we-are-paying-now?}

The following is the breakdown of different instance types we are paying for in EMR clusters. Note that we are paying the most for the most undesirable instance types c7g.16xlarge, and c6g.16xlarge, which also generate the most waste of resources.

![][image8]

## Why does the memory-to-cpu ratio matter for the EC2 instance types? {#why-does-the-memory-to-cpu-ratio-matter-for-the-ec2-instance-types?}

We have configured all Spark jobs with a default configuration of [spark.executor.core=1](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/blob/master/emradm/data/emr/base.json#L131), and [spark.executor.memory=4G](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/blob/master/emradm/data/emr/base.json#L134). This means that the minimum memory-to-cpu ratio is 4\. As seen in the case studies, we have many Spark jobs with much higher ratios, as high as 20\. The YARN scheduler can only allocate resources on the container level. Ideally we should use EC2 instances with the ratio that matches closely to our job’s ratio. Obviously we can’t find an EC2 instance with the ratio of 20, but we can at least shoot for not ever getting EC2 instances with ratio less than 4\.

To give an example, let’s say we actually get our desirable instances with a ratio of 8\. That is, r6g.16xlarge, which has 64 cores, and 512 GB memory. If we run the workload with a ratio of 20, it will fit 25 executors per instance at most (512/20=25.6), but these 25 executors only consume 25 cores, so the rest of 39 CPU cores are wasted, which accounts for 60%. 

Now let’s look at the worst case, if we run this workload on the most spot instances we get a low ratio of 2\. Say c6g.16xlarge, which comes with 128GB memory and 64 vcores. Each such machine can fit 6 executors, and these executors will use 6 cores. The remaining 58 cores are wasted, which account for 90% waste.

This is the root cause why the CPU usage on EMR clusters is so low. The actual CPU waste is probably somewhere between 50% to 90% because we do have some workload that use the default ratio of 4 and YARN may schedule these executors on the same instance with the higher ratio ones.

## Why should we set spark.executor.cores to 4? {#why-should-we-set-spark.executor.cores-to-4?}

This is because it is a best practice to use 4 cores per Spark executor to max out the IO throughput. More cores will not bring further throughput improvement, but less cores per executor means less throughput. Longer IO wait could mean less efficiency for memory and CPU, because the memorySeconds and cpuSeconds accumulated during the wait are all resources we can save by max out the IO bottleneck.

Also, the total number of concurrent tasks each Spark executor can run is determined by this configuration. Setting it to 4 means the executor can run 4 tasks in parallel. There are also situations where we can get better performance by running more tasks per executor. For example, based on Spark’s unified memory model, sometimes we can get more memory usage efficiency with more concurrent tasks. As an example, broadcast join will broadcast a small dataset to each executor. If the executor runs 4 tasks, all 4 tasks will share the same copy of the broadcasted dataset. With today’s limit of 1 core per executor, we have to broadcast and consume 4x memory space to hold this data, with ¾ of which can be saved.

Increasing this number to 4 should be taken by also increasing the spark.executor.memory configurations to mitigate potential OOM risks.

## What instance type do you recommend to replace the on-demand nodes? {#what-instance-type-do-you-recommend-to-replace-the-on-demand-nodes?}

Based on the weekly used vcore chart above, in the past 7 days, we peaked at using a total of 40k vcores (at timestamp 2/13 2:30pm) across 3 data-platform-emr6 clusters. Ideally, we should have historical metrics to help us to decide on the ideal memory-to-cpu ratio.  To make some assumptions, we can assume that we want a ratio between the range of 5 \- 10\. I think the **r6g** instance family (or r7g for that matter) is a good starting point. We plan to invest in better Spark metrics and observability in the future so we can make more data-driven decisions.

## How many instances do we need for TASK nodes? {#how-many-instances-do-we-need-for-task-nodes?}

See [discussion here](#bookmark=kix.8mo2kanr412t), we can replace the 2000 spot nodes of unpredictable sizes and waste, with 200 on-demand nodes, provide the same throughput (based on the number of vcores), but may cost less (because of the higher memory-to-cpu ratio) and more reliable (80% reduction on cluster size reduces the workload on the ResourceManager). This reduction of cluster size also begs the question if we should merge all of them into one cluster with about a total of 1200 nodes.

[image6]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image7]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image8]: <data:image/png;base64,>
